coredata.dc:title: H2 production from ammonia borane hydrolysis with catalyst effect of Titriplex® III carbon quantum dots supported by ruthenium under different reactant Conditions: Experimental study and predictions with molecular modelling

coredata.dc:description: This study presents experimental and theoretical results on the role of the catalytic effect by ruthenium added to titriplex® III Carbon Quantum Dots (CQD) support material in the hydrolysis of ammonia borane for H2-production. H2 production was achieved under different reactant conditions (in variable amounts of catalysis, ruthenium, NaOH, ammonia borane and at various temperature). Since the experimental results and theoretical calculations which includes the effect of all electrical forces acting on valence electrons (overlap density functions, van der Waals forces, Covalent bonding and halogen bonds) are in very good agreement in the study, some theoretical predictions are also made. By considering the rhodium element instead of the ruthenium element, the performance of the titriplex support material was estimated to be approximately 30% more effective than ruthenium. The NaOH molecule acts as a hydrolyzed water molecule with catalysis interaction and contributes to the formation of oxidized boron. When NaOH is not present, the interaction time of the ammonia borane molecule with catalysis comes to the fore. This time is called threshold time. In molecular modeling, while almost all of the ammonia borane molecules contribute to H production, only 15% of the water molecules contribute to this rate. However, while some of the released H atoms remains in the water, other parts observe to play a role in the production of H2 and hydronium. The interaction rate of the water molecule increases with the increase in the interaction surface of the catalyst. Any external effect that prevents the formation of hydronium and accelerates the interaction of free H atoms with each other increases the efficiency. One of the predicted results in this study is that the increased pressure shows separating role for hydronium.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents experimental and theoretical results on the role of the catalytic effect by ruthenium added to titriplex® III Carbon Quantum Dots (CQD) support material in the hydrolysis of ammonia borane for H2-production. H2 production was achieved under different reactant conditions (in variable amounts of catalysis, ruthenium, NaOH, ammonia borane and at various temperature). Since the experimental results and theoretical calculations which includes the effect of all electrical forces acting on valence electrons (overlap density functions, van der Waals forces, Covalent bonding and halogen bonds) are in very good agreement in the study, some theoretical predictions are also made. By considering the rhodium element instead of the ruthenium element, the performance of the titriplex support material was estimated to be approximately 30% more effective than ruthenium. The NaOH molecule acts as a hydrolyzed water molecule with catalysis interaction and contributes to the formation of oxidized boron. When NaOH is not present, the interaction time of the ammonia borane molecule with catalysis comes to the fore. This time is called threshold time. In molecular modeling, while almost all of the ammonia borane molecules contribute to H production, only 15% of the water molecules contribute to this rate. However, while some of the released H atoms remains in the water, other parts observe to play a role in the production of H2 and hydronium. The interaction rate of the water molecule increases with the increase in the interaction surface of the catalyst. Any external effect that prevents the formation of hydronium and accelerates the interaction of free H atoms with each other increases the efficiency. One of the predicted results in this study is that the increased pressure shows separating role for hydronium.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para:
As known, environmental degradation has recently occurred with the excessive consumption of fossil fuels, and it causes climate change [1–6]. Therefore, the transition to a cleaner, sustainable and environmentally friendly energy system comes to the fore with hydrogen energy. Because hydrogen energy is 3 times more efficient when compared to fossil fuels [7,8]. However, there are problems regarding the production and safe storage of hydrogen and its use in vehicles [9–12]. Therefore, the development of suitable hydrogen storage materials and technologies appears to be an important factor in the application of hydrogen energy [13,14]. Chemical hydrides as hydrogen providers are sodium borohydride (NaBH4) [15–18], ammonia borane NH3BH3 [19–22], potassium borohydride (KBH4) [23–26], lithium borohydride (LiBH4) [27], (CH3)2NHBH3 [28,29]. Among these hydrides, NH3BH3 has recently attracted great attention for the chemical storage of hydrogen due to its high hydrogen storage content (19.6 %), excellent stability and non-toxicity [8,30–38]. Since ammonia borane does not hydrolyze spontaneously, it can release three moles of hydrogen molecules at ambient temperature in the presence of suitable catalysts [39]. These are obtained through hydrolysis, alcoholysis [40–43], and thermolysis [44]. The reaction of ammonia borane in the presence of suitable catalysts occurs as follows [45].
(1)

Most of the catalysts used so far for the hydrolysis of ammonia borane are heterogeneous metal catalysts [14]. The reason for using heterogeneous catalysts is that these catalysts are preferred due to their reusability, durability and being more economical. These metal heterogeneous catalysts are Co, Ni, Fe, Cr based catalysts [20,38,46–49]. However, when such catalysts are used without support material, the components come together and agglomerate, which significantly reduces the activity of the catalyst. Therefore, recently graphene oxide [49–51], zeolite [52], ZIF [53], carbon quantum dots (CQD) [54–57], activated carbon [54,58], polymer-based support materials, Al2O3 [59], metal–organic materials and TiO2 are used as support materials for the catalyst.

In recent studies, ruthenium-based catalysts have attracted attention due to their lower costs and their increased activity after adding with suitable support materials [60]. Recent studies have shown that ruthenium (III) chloride hydrate and ruthenium (III) acetylacetonate act as homogeneous catalysts in the production of hydrogen from the hydrolysis of boron hydrides at room temperature [61–64].

As support material in this study, carbon quantum dots obtained from titriplex® III (TCQD) for analysis (ethyleneditrilotetraacetic acid, disodium salt dehydrates) were synthesized. As known, carbon quantum dots stand out because they have very small diameters (3–4 nm) and fast electron transfer, inclusion of functional groups such as NH2, and a high surface area.[65–69]. However, since they are produced under hydrothermal conditions [70], that is, by green synthesis, they have important features such as being both environmentally friendly and low-cost [68,71,72]. Titriplex® III here is chosen due to the presence of the OH, N and O structures mostly found in the structure. Thanks to the high electron density in these structures and the van der Waals bonds formed between the Ru atoms, the Ru atoms gain a very large surface area and the catalytic effect increases. This situation here has been confirmed by the experimental and theoretical data of the study. Here, optimum conditions are determined for the hydrogens obtained as a result of the hydrolysis of ammonia borane in the presence of the catalyst Ru@TCQD, according to different NaOH mass concentrations, catalyst amount and ammonia borane concentrations. In addition, in order to determine its kinetics, the degree of reaction and activation energy are determined in the light of the data obtained from hydrolysis at different temperatures. In addition, to determine the durability of the catalyst, the same catalyst was reused 6 times under the same conditions. H2 production from NH3BH3 hydrolysis with Titriplex® III (CQD) catalyst supported with Ru was first time studied. The characterization of the catalyst was investigated with ultraviolet, TEM, Fluorescence, ICP-OES and FT-IR analyses. In addition to these conditions, H2 molecule rate was theoretically examined by using the rhodium element instead of the ruthenium element. When looking at the molecular dynamics results of the contribution of the rhodium element in homogeneous environments and in a slightly higher pressure environment, some predictions were made. The rhodium activity of the relevant carbon quantum dot structure was compared with ruthenium. Theoretical calculations present an important goal to better analyze dynamic movements that are almost impossible to observe experimentally, such as femtoseconds. This study proved that some predictions can be made without incurring experimental costs. Hydrogen production rates obtained by the hydrolysis of ammonia borane in the presence of a catalyst and the initial cycle frequencies were calculated and compared with the other studies. TOF value at 25 °C is 133.4 molH2.molRu -1.min−1 (hydrogen production rate is 37740 mL.g−1.min−1), TOF value at 30 °C is 172.9 molH2.molRu -1.min−1 (hydrogen production rate is 47472 mL.g−1.min−1), the TOF value at 50 °C was 267.2 molH2.molRu -1.min−1 (hydrogen production rate is 159275 mL.g−1.min−1), the TOF value increased 18 times compared to the initial value, and the hydrogen production rate increased more than 4 times. It was determined that the synthesized catalyst shows better performance when compared to the Ru-based catalysts synthesized in the literature (from the perspective of TOF and hydrogen production rates). It was also determined that the catalyst had a reaction degree of the nth order (0.66) and an activation energy of 39.76 kJ/mol.

In the rest of the work, the next section contains detailed information on the preparation of Ru@TCQD, catalytic hydrolysis of ammonia borane and theoretical modeling. In the next section, the experimental and theoretical results are compared.

Merck − Titriplex® III (99,9%) for analysis (ethylenedinitrilotetraacetic acid, disodium salt dihydrate) was purchased ACS, ISO, Reag. Ruthenium (III) chloride hydrate (RuCl3.XH2O) (99,8%), ethanol (CH3CH2OH) (99,8%), methanol (CH3OH) (99,8%), borane ammonia complex (NH3BH3) (97 %) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) (98 %) were purchased Sigma-Aldrich and sodium borohydride fine granular (NaBH4) (99 %) was purchased via Merck Co. Ltd. All chemicals are commercially available and are used without further refinement.

5 Mmol Titriplex® III was placed in a 100 ml autoclave containing 60 ml of pure water and placed in the oven. CQD production was carried out by keeping the autoclave at 180 °C for 10 h. The produced DOTs were filtered and then 40 ml of methanol was added to 40 ml of DOT solution. The presence of the produced CQDs was tested by comparing daylight and 360 nm UV light. After CQD production, Ru doping process was started. 0.4 mmol Ruthenium(III) chloride hydrate salt was added to the DOT solution containing the same ratio of pure water and methanol. It was stirred at 1000 rpm for 24 h under room conditions so that metal atoms gain charge [73]. After charging metal atoms, 15 mmol NaBH4 was dissolved in 20 ml of pure water and methanol in the same ratio. The reduction process was carried out by dropping NaBH4 solution into the DOT-Ru solution mixed at 1000 rpm under room conditions. It was observed that there was very little precipitation in the catalyst structure after waiting for 60 h. The catalyst structure was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 45 min and the homogeneous part was removed by decantation. The Ru ratio in the homogeneous structure was determined by ICP-MS

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi High-Tech, HT 7700) are employed to characterize the morphology of the samples. UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra are recorded using a SHIMADZU UV2550 spectrometer across a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. Fluorescence values of carbon quantum dots are measured with a Perkin Elmer LS-55 Fluorescence Spectrometer. The precise content of Ru in the catalyst is analyzed on an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Agilent ICP-AES730).

The amount of hydrogen obtained from the hydrolysis of ammonia borane is determined by the water displacement method. Experiments are carried out by placing the reaction at 303 K, 32 mg of catalyst, 10 ml of deionized water containing 1 % NaOH, into the glass reactor and adding 1 mmol of ammonia borane on the magnetic stirrer. For the hydrogen obtained as a result of the hydrolysis of ammonia borane in the presence of Ru@TCQD, the optimum conditions of different NaOH mass concentration, different amount of catalyst, and different ammonia borane concentration are determined, and also the degree of reaction and activation energy are determined in the light of the data obtained from the hydrolysis at different temperatures to determine its kinetics. Hydrogen production rate is determined by the formula below.
(2)

( m L )

Here; r is the production rate of hydrogen, “VH2″ is the volume (mL) of Hydrogen obtained, ”g cat“ is the amount of catalyst used, and ”min“ is the time spent during ammonia borane hydrolysis. Additionally, the recycling stability of the Ru@TCQD catalyst in the presence of ammonia borane hydrolysis is measured six times under the same conditions. Experiments are carried out by injecting ammonia borane solution (1 mM, 10 mL) and 32 mg Ru@TCQD catalyst into the reactor after hydrogen production each time.

It is impossible to calculate exact quantum mechanically large systems containing large numbers of atoms and molecular systems. For this reason, some approaches are needed in calculating the energy surfaces that form the motion field of atoms in dynamic calculations. The fastest way to do this is to make semi-empirical calculations using experimental parameters. So, the tight-binding Hamiltonian, first described by Grimme et al (GFNxTB) [74], is used to achieve charge convergence. This model uses semi-empirical parameters when calculating non-covalent interactions. Approximations are available in the Hamiltonian operator that represents the system, also known as the eigenvalue equation. The only difference between the quantum mechanical DFTB3 model, which takes into account third-order charge derivatives and the non-Scc-GFN1-xTB model is the differences in the Eel expression in Equation (3). The common feature in all models is that the total Hamiltonian energy, the sum of electronic energy (indexed by “el”), repulsive coulomb interaction (indexed by “rep”), dispersion energy (indexed by “disp”) and halogen-bonding (indexed by “XB”) the terms:
(3)
E =
E

+
E
rep
+
E
disp
+
E
XB
Here, E value is also the total energy value. It consists of the sum of the parameters that contribute to this energy value. The part that creates difficulties in the calculations here is the Eel contribution. In order to show the third degree contributions of this contribution, a distribution as in Equation (4) is realized.
(4)

where the first term is the total energy of occupied valance orbitals. Next two terms represent the contribution of the second and third energy derivatives, respectively. The last one represents the sum of the energy of the free electrons [74]. The last term includes the kinetic energy of free electrons. However, in the current molecular dynamics calculation, this value is equal to zero because there is no free electron. The tight-binding approach, considering the interaction between two atoms, has a feature that prevents computational cost once compared to the DFT model. The DFT model covers the movements of all electrons in the system. In the Non-Scc-GFN1xTB model used in this study, the Hamiltonian energy is determined with the Slater Atomic Orbital function used in the Extended Hünkel Theory instead of Mulliken charges terms. In this case, there is no need to use the second and third terms in Equation (4). In this case, the computational cost is significantly reduced. These unaccounted for energy gradients require a tight-binding approach. It makes an important contribution to molecular dissociation and molecular formations, preventing some self-consistency charge failures. This model also shows a very good approach to the optimization of transition metals. Since the non-Scc-GFN1xTB model does not give good results in weak halogen bonds, the fourth term showing halogen contributions makes an important contribution in this study [74–77]. These last three terms have been discussed in detail in previous work by some of the authors of the study [78]. All calculations in this study are made with the Amsterdam Modeling Suite (AMS) [77] software, which allows the use of the approach described above. The computational cell is created by applying periodic boundary conditions in three dimensions. The appropriate Maxwell-Boltzmann velocity distribution for the desired temperature is used to determine the initial atomic velocities. The unit time step is set to 0.25 fs. The canonical (NVT) by the constant-temperature, constant-volume ensemble is used to describe the thermodynamic states of the model system. In all simulations, the velocities of the atoms in the MD cell are scaled and the Berendsen thermostat algorithm is preferred for temperature control. The system is simulated at 25 °C and 50 °C up to equilibrium state. The equations of motion are solved by using the predictor- corrector algorithm in determining the trajectory of the particles over a time step of 0.25 fs.

The CQD production process was tested with daylight and 360 nm UV light. Daylight and 360 nm UV light images are given in inside part of Fig. 1 . Once the fluorescence feature of the produced material structure is understood, the absorbance values of the liquid samples are calculated using UV–VIS and UV fluorescence. The results regarding these absorbances are given in Fig. 1. It is seen that CQD has an absorption value of 1.728 Å. It is seen that the absorption value decreases to 0.456 Å after ruthenium is doped. This shows that ruthenium causes changes in electronic transitions through the hypochromic effect. Through the doping process, ruthenium atoms interact with CQD ligands, leading to changes in the energy transitions [79,80]. Ruthenium is generally bound to π electron systems in organic molecules [25,8]. Through the doping process, ruthenium is included in the π electron system in the CQD and in this case, the changes in the π-π* transitions occur. The highest absorption band π-π* of both samples is detected at 274 nm. This means that Ru metal atoms are stably attached to the CQD surface. Fluorescence (PL) excitation was performed between 300 and 445 nm in steps of 5 nm. While CQDs give the strongest excitation at 364 nm, Ru-doped CQDs give the strongest excitation at 314 nm [81].

FT-IR and TEM analyzes are carried out to reveal the morphologies and structures of homogeneous nanocomposites synthesized by the hydrothermal method of nano-sized Titriplex III. Fig. 2 shows CQD/Titriplex III (a), TEM images of CQD@Ru before hydrolysis (b), TEM images of CQD@Ru after repeated use (c–d), and FT-IR results of Ru and Ru@CQD material structures (e). It is seen that the average particle sizes of CQD/Titriplex, CQD@Ru before hydrolysis and CQD@Ru after repeated use are 2.5, 24 and 30 nm, respectively. It is understood from Fig. 2 (a) that the CQDs obtained from titriplex III by the hydrothermal method have a very small structure of homogeneous size. However, the average size uniformity in CQD particles increases slightly after Ru loading. This must be related to the amount of Ru added. At this point, the amount of Ru that CQDs can bind homogeneously is important. Excessive Ru addition increases the metal–metal interaction, shifting from homogeneity to heterogeneity [82]. The TEM image of this situation after repeated use is seen in Fig. 2 (c-d). After the 3rd repeated use of the experimental application, a low density heterogeneity due to metal–metal interaction is observed. The intensity of this heterogeneous appearance increases slightly after the 6th use. This shows that there is a very delicate balance between homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts. It should be noted here that the study was carried out at values ​​between completely homogeneous catalyst limits, except for repeated uses.

The analysis results of Ru(0) and Ru@CQD nanoclusters, for which FT-IR analysis was performed to understand the interaction in the material structure after Ru-CQD combination and repeated use, are as shown in Fig. 2-e. It is seen that the peaks between 1800–2400 cm−1 of Ru(0) nanoclusters largely disappear after CQD incorporation. After the combination of Ru and CQD, it is seen that the O/N-H peaks starting at 3338 cm−1 merge with the C-H peaks at 2932 cm−1, confirming the C, H, N and O groups in the Titriplex III structure in a wide band range. It is evaluated that the peaks at 1701 cm−1, 1397 cm−1 and 1099 cm−1 in the catalyst structure confirm the C=O, B-C and Ru-CQD bindings, respectively. It is seen that after repeated use of the catalyst, the material structure does not deteriorate and there is a decrease in B-C and Ru-CQD vibrations. The possible reason for this situation is the dominance of metal–metal interaction due to repeated use [83,84]. The diameter increase in nanoclusters due to increasing metal interaction is clearly seen in Fig. 2 c and d.

The effectiveness of the prepared catalyst is determined as a result of the hydrolysis of ammonia borane. The experiments are carried out with a controlled experimental system using variable parameters such as temperature, mixing speed, ammonia borane concentration, NaOH mass concentration, and catalyst amount. Here, in order to determine the performance of the catalyst, the effect of amounts of NaOH on the hydrolysis of ammonia borane for the alkalinity of the solution is examined, since ammonia borane hydrolyzes spontaneously at room temperature.

In Fig. 3 , considering that the fixed parameters in the solution are 298 K, 1 mmol ammonia borane and 16 mg catalyst, the effect of different NaOH mass concentrations on the hydrolysis of ammonia borane is examined. In the same Figure, the effect of the amount of NaOH on the initial H2 production rates is examined. As can be seen from the Figure, the hydrogen production rate without NaOH is 4963 ml/min, while at 1 % NaOH mass concentration, this value increases approximately 9 times. It is seen that the hydrogen production rate decreases at higher concentrations. The catalyst plays a role in the separation of H2 by forming a complex with AB. As can be seen from Fig. 3, the low density of NaOH in the environment positively supports the formation of H2 after the complex. The optimum amount of NaOH for the Ru@TCQD catalyst was determined as 1 %. Here, a significant increase in the reaction rate was observed in the presence of NaOH. Since NaOH can react with many molecular groups in a wide range of areas during the hydrolysis process, it has a great role as a trigger for the reaction. NaOH plays a very important role in such studies because it is an ionic compound that can be easily dissolved and homogenized in water and can easily produce H2 at the end of the reaction. However, using more than a certain amount depending on the ratio of reactants is not very effective in H2 production. Because each NaOH molecule releases Na+ into the reaction medium and H+ as the resulting product, after a certain rate, base saturation occurs depending on the ratio of the reactants. After a certain increase value, the Na cation block develops in the system. This situation can be understood more clearly in molecular dynamics simulation. However, in the simulation, this phenomenon was examined as a system with and without NaOH. The NaOH ratio was simulated at ratio of 1 %, which gives the best H2 production experimentally. In order to evaluate the amount of NaOH in other ratios, it is necessary to use more molecular groups in the reactants. In such a large-scale system, very long time propagations would be needed to provide this population. This is almost impossible even according to the semi-empirical calculation used in the theoretical simulation. Fig. 4 shows the dynamic results with and without NaOH.

As can be seen from this simulation, when NaOH is not in the system, H2 production starts late, approximately 0.25 ps (threshold time). On the other hand, almost half of the total production (up to 4 ps) has been realized in NaOH solution. It is seen that, in the presence of NaOH in the system, the total production is approximately 4 times higher up to 4 ps. In cases after 4 ps, H2 production requires a longer statistical collision time due to the decreasing number of free H atoms in the solution. Due to the ratio of atoms in the system, no effective increase or decrease in this total number of H2 is expected. In the molecular dynamics system, free H atoms exhibit dynamic behavior by being controlled under periodic boundary conditions with random distribution. However, according to the theory mentioned in the modeling section, free H atoms approaching each other statistically first gain binding energy with dispersion energy, which plays a role in long-distance interactions. Therefore, the number of free H atoms decreases. In an environment where there are few H atoms, the interaction statistics of H atoms with each other decreases[85] The molecules used here are 4 titriplex-III, 7 Ru, 21 NH3BH3, 14 NaOH (for NaOH solution) and 300 H2O. In Fig. 5 , a drawing with 300 H2O invisible feature was recorded from the NaOH-free system for a detailed view. In Fig. 5, only NH3BH3, titriplex-III and Ru doped on titriplex-III are visible.

In the system whose initial simulation looks like Fig. 5, except for the H2O molecules, at the end of the simulation, there are 7 free H atoms and 23 OH molecules in the solution environment. While the entire NH3BH3 molecule are involved in this hydrolysis event, only 15 % (about 40 H2O molecules) of the water molecules contribute. It should be noted that the increase of the OH molecule is proportional to the decrease rate of the NH3BH3 molecule. At the end of the simulation, there are 16 NH3BH2 molecules. While 8 free H atoms form hydronium ion, free oxygen atoms oxidize the boron. There are 8 oxygen atoms release at the end of the simulation. Looking at these values, it seems that if the time-dependent propagation is extended a little more, the H2 molecules continues to emerge. However, considering the total number of particles, a statistically long period of time is needed.

In the next step 298 K, 1 mmol ammonia borane, 1 % NaOH are the parameters kept constant. Here the effect of the change in the amount of catalyst on the hydrolysis of ammonia borane is examined. Fig. 6 shows the hydrogen production rate depending on the amount of catalyst to obtain hydrogen from the hydrolysis of ammonia borane. It is seen that as the amount of catalyst increases, the hydrogen production rate decreases after the presence of 0.8 mg Ru. It can be said that the reason of this depends on the catalyst per solution. Another reason is the homogeneous distribution of nanoparticles for the catalyst to be obtained in optimum doping of Ru. It also increases catalytic activity because it facilitates high-speed electron transfer. It is considered that the increase in the amount of Ru increases the metal–metal interaction and causes agglomeration. This situation is predicted to be the main reason for decreasing catalytic activity [86]. In catalytic optimization, H2 efficiency reaches its maximum at the best values ​​of the catalyst, support material and H2 source. In this study, the value in question was obtained in the amount of CQD by adding 0.8 mg Ru.

Theoretically, increase in the amount of ruthenium is in good agreement with the experiment. The change in the amount of Ru is considered as 1 Ru, 7 Ru and 10 Ru in the dynamic simulation up to 2 ps (a meaningful molecular dynamics time interval evaluated with the number of atoms it contains) as seen in Fig. 7 . H2 production in 1 Ru system is the product of the performance of the active surface of only a single Ru element. It shows that base saturation occurs around the Ru element at approximately 1.5 ps. This is due to the fact that the H2 providers around Ru are far from the electrostatic interaction field in a small nanoscale system. To follow the increase in H2 production, it is necessary to wait for a much longer period of time. However, even in such a system, processor time is quite costly. At 7 Ru, both total H2 production and rate increase. While 1 Ru provides a steady state in the production of 8 H2 at 1.3 ps, in the case of 7 Ru it provides almost stability in the production of 20 H2 at 0.5 ps. However, it provides full steady state in approximately 1.5 ps. This delay in stability is related to the rate of change in the surface area of ​​the agglomerated Ru elements over time. At 10 Ru, the dynamic flow of an additional 3 Ru elements is seen in the part “a” of Fig. 7. According to the logic of molecular dynamics movement, all possible interactions of the elements in the system are tried to be ensured and the system always tends to be in a stable state. Therefore, increasing the number of Ru elements should work in parallel with increasing the number of titriplex molecules. If Titriplex is kept constant, there will be no support material for free Ru elements to travel through and Ru elements will interact with each other. Here are photos of some dynamic times for 10 Ru. It can be seen that up to approximately 0.15 ps, 3 Ru elements are still actively circulating on the titriplex surface (the part “b” of the figure). These additional 3 Ru elements appear to provide faster H2 production than 7 Ru during this time period. However, if agglomeration begins, H2 production is suppressed. It is clearly seen that at the breaking point in this production, the agglomeration process begins and this process varies over time (the part “c” of the figure). The time-dependent decrease in the catalytic activity region also reduces the possibility of other H2 providers that are candidate interactors to access the catalytic activity region over time. With increasing Ru, unless the support material increases, Ru-Ru interaction becomes inevitable and reduces the current activity. Unless the support material surface increases, the added ruthenium elements is bond with each other and aggregate. If there is no external factor (such as temperature and pressure) that can affect this bonding, the molecules in the ground electronic state, which tend to form a stable structure, approach each other over time. In this case, it is necessary to re-evaluate the concept of homogeneity. Aggregating Ru elements create a solid phase in its own volume. The homogeneous distribution of Ru turns into heterogeneous distribution with increasing Ru elements.

To test the optimal conditions, the effect of different concentrations of ammonia borane on the Ru@TCQD catalyst is examined. Fig. 8 shows the effect of 4 different ammonia borane concentrations on hydrogen production using the Ru@TCQD catalyst, and the effect of different ammonia borane concentrations on hydrogen production rates is given in the graph at inside part the Figure. As can be seen from the Figure, total hydrogen production increase as the amount of ammonia borane concentration increase. In the hydrogen production rate graph of different ammonia borane concentrations, it is seen that as the amount of ammonia borane concentration increases, the hydrogen production rate increases, unlike other borohydrides. In other words, it is observed that in the hydrolysis of most borohydrides, as the concentration of borohydride increases, the hydrogen production rate decreases depending on the solution viscosity. The reason why this does not happen in the catalyst produced here is that the present catalyst is homogeneous and the borohydride used is ammonia borane.

The first interaction of ammonia borane in the solution environment begins with the hydrolysis of the H atoms of the boron atom. This occurs faster with the catalytic effect or the presence of NaOH molecules in the solution. The H atoms in the water molecules and the H atoms bonded to the boron atom are released for the production of H2. Thus, the boron atom can be oxidized according to the atomic distribution around it. The boron element in ammonia borane, which is about to completely lose its H atoms, can leave one last H atom to the Nitrogen atom while leaving the N atom. NH4 can be seen as the product molecule in this process. With the presence of a catalyst or heat, H atoms bonded to the nitrogen atom also contribute to the production of H2. As mentioned above, the decrease in the amount of ammonia borane increases the free OH ratio. In this case, if the catalyst can interact with the entire ammonia borane system in the aqueous solution, that is, if it spreads homogeneously in the system, the released OH molecules might interact with the boron atom in a very short time. In such a case, the H2 production rate increase. Thus, the increase in the number of ammonia borane increases H2-production [87,88]. This causes a dramatic decrease in both the rate of H2 production and its total amount. We would like to point out that the significant increase in H2 production rate in the increase of ammonia borane in Fig. 8 is completely related to the catalysis structure. Although different mechanisms have been proposed regarding the decomposition reaction mechanism of AB, it is the accepted basic fact that the decomposition reaction proceeds through NH2BH2. Apart from this reality, there is no clear accepted reaction mechanism. However, the accepted decomposition mechanism of some stages of the complex formed by the metal (M) structure in the catalyst in the presence of a homogeneous catalyst in the presence of the homogeneous catalyst given in Equation (1) is as in Equation (5–7) [89].

As is known, the stability and reusability of the catalyst play an important role in practical applications. The reusability of the Ru@TCQD catalyst six times under the same conditions as ammonia borane hydrolysis and the ratio of its catalytic activity (% conversion) are shown in Fig. 9 . As can be seen from the figure, the ratio (mol H2/mol ammonia borane) is almost the same in all 6 uses depending on time, only the hydrolysis time increases. One of the possible reasons for this situation is that the homogeneity in the catalyst shifts to heterogeneity due to metal–metal interaction as a result of repeated use. Because the main difference that distinguishes homogeneous catalyst from heterogeneous catalyst is the reaction rate. In this study, the decrease in the percentage of reaction rate as a result of repeated use is one of the best examples of the decrease due to the shift to heterogeneity. In the % conversion rates given in Fig. 9, it is seen that there is 100 % conversion after 6 uses. This shows that there is no serious change in the phase structures of the obtained Ru@TCQD catalyst. FT-IR analysis results in Fig. 2-e confirm this situation. This also shows that until the 6th use, no accumulation occurred in the particle size of the catalyst that would eliminate the activity. This means that the amount of Titriplex and Ru selected for the molecular dynamics of reusability is consistent.

Catalysis with a system consisting of only a few hundred atoms is sufficient to understand the behavior of atoms. Fig. 10 shows the tendency of Ru elements interacting with each other depending on time. Since the structure consists of 4 titriplex molecular groups, in such a small isolated system, Ru elements can bend the structure as shown in Fig. 10. Thus, a carbon barrier is formed around Ru elements. This is the state after 2 ps. (It should be emphasized here that there is a situation of up to 2 ps for 10 Ru mentioned in Fig. 7.). The time factor can be seen as reuse in such studies. In such a state, the first behavior that suppresses H2 production is the aggregation of Ru elements as seen in Fig. 7. This agglomeration is the most important factor affecting the H2 production rate. In order to talk about long-term reuse in such small-structured systems, it is necessary to draw attention to the deformation in the structure. This deformation is a process that occurs after the agglomeration process and depends on the stability of the metal ion and support material. Since the structure is small, deformation in the titriplex molecular system manifests itself in a very short time. In such a theoretical simulation, for long-term processes to be associated with reuse, the system must contain more groups of atoms (For example, instead of 4 titriplex, 8 titriplex molecular groups can be simulated). However, increasing the number of atoms creates the problem of computational cost factor in the system. It shows that it is pointless to see more time steps in such simulations. Looking at Fig. 14, the H2 change is almost meaningless when the simulation is kept up to 14 ps.

To investigate the catalytic kinetics of the Ru@TCQD catalyst, ammonia borane hydrolysis is studied depending on temperature between 298 K and 323 K. Fig. 11 shows the experimental hydrolysis of ammonia borane in the presence of Ru@TCQD catalyst at different temperatures. As can be seen from the Figure, the catalytic reaction rate of ammonia borane hydrolysis increases with increasing temperature. When the amount of catalysis, ammonia borane and NaOH used in the solution is kept constant, only the temperature parameter is changed. In the results that show significant dependence on temperature, when compared only 25 and 50 degrees Celsius, the amount of H2 that is reached at 25 degrees in 1-minute reaches in a quarter of the time at 50 degrees. Again, when looked at the 4th minute, it reaches the H2 production that it reaches at 25 degrees, four times faster at 50 degrees (in the 1st minute). This ratio, on average, coincides with the theoretical results in Fig. 12 . H2 production at 0.5 ps in the system at 25 degrees is almost the same as around 0.125 ps at 50 degrees. This first spike region is the most significant region in these theoretical studies. Ratios of approximately 4-fold can also be seen at other time points. However, the region where there is not much change over time, known as the plateau region, is statistically related to the collision situations of free H atoms. In this region, there is generally an increase over time. In some cases, free H2 can be separated according to the interaction environment. This is related to the approximations of the parameters used by the model used in H-H bonding. However, the H2 separated in the system becomes stable again. Another region in the theoretical approach is the 0.1 ps region related to the first time steps. Here, an equal increase is observed in both temperatures. The increase in collision kinetics provided by the increase in temperature in the system brings to the fore the interaction time in the system. This value, which is related to the system density, is related to the mean free paths. In this case, the full transfer of the temperature increase to kinetic energy occurred after this value.

In addition, the volume values obtained depending on time at different temperatures are drawn by the linear fitting method of the Arrhenius curve. According to the Arrhenius equation in Fig. 13 , the activation energy (Ea) can be calculated using Equation (8). When the graph of lnK versus 1/T is drawn, the activation energy is determined to be 39.76 kJ/mol.
(8)
lnK = l n A -
Ea
RT
Here, “A” represents the frequency factor, “k” represents the rate constant, “R” represents the gas constant, and “T” represents the reaction temperature (K).
The comparison of TOF value and activation energy values with the literature is shown in Table 1 .

The model used, as described above, is an ideal theory for observing H2 production. It also supports the dissociation of H atoms, ignoring some of the disadvantages of the self-consistency charge model. Clarifying the experimentally incomprehensible aspects of femto-second system behavior in theoretical simulations is the most impressive and advantageous aspect of theoretical studies. This is also the main purpose of theoretical studies. In addition, the fact that this model can give more meaningful results in transition metals allows more realistic results to be achieved in catalysis studies. Due to the perfect consistency between the above experimental and theoretical studies, some tests that are experimentally not carried out (both to decrease budget costs and to save time) are investigated theoretically. In Fig. 14 , the effect of 6 Ru (ruthenium) and 6 Rh (rhodium) on H2 production are compared according to some initial environmental conditions. Rhodium is much more effective than ruthenium in terms of both reaction rate and total H2 production amount. It is predicted that the Titriplex III molecule may have a better catalysis effect with the rhodium element. Rh elements, like Ru, were distributed homogeneously on the titriplex support material.

If a distinction between homogeneity and heterogeneity is desired, more titriplex support material and catalysis active elements in relevant proportions must be used. Thus, the agglomerated metal elements are mentioned. Considering that even in a system consisting of such hundreds of atoms (in which there are only 300 H2O molecules, excluding other molecules), a task takes several weeks, it would be remarkable to impose heterogeneity in the most basic unit of such a system. However, experimental cost turns into computational cost. Also, system behavior is examined when an increase of 25 % pressure is applied to 6 rhodium systems. It is observed that the pressure factor, just like temperature, causes an increase in both the H2 production rate and the H2 production amount. It is observed that even in the region called the plateau, the free H atoms which have the interaction statistics with each other increase.

This is a pioneering study that demonstrates a very good fit between experimental and modelling. In catalysis studies that require high experimental costs and time, getting some predictions without experimenting (with modeling) is important in terms of both reducing of costs and preventing to loss of time. Another advantage of molecular modeling clarifies events that are not observed experimentally at the femto-second level. In this study, the effect of catalysis on ammonia borane hydrolysis when ruthenium is doped into titriplex III support material is examined. It is shown in molecular dynamics simulation how the presence of NaOH molecule in the system reduces the threshold energy in the system. The NaOH molecule acts as a hydrolyzed water molecule that has undergone catalysis and contributes to the formation of oxidized boron. When NaOH is not present, the interaction time of the ammonia borane molecule with catalysis comes to the fore. This time is called threshold time. In molecular modeling, while almost all of the ammonia borane molecules contribute to H production, only 15 % of the water molecules contribute to this rate. However, while some of the released H atoms remain in the water, other parts are observed to play a role in the production of H2 and hydronium. The interaction rate of the water molecule increases with the increase in the interaction surface of the catalyst. Any external effect that prevents the formation of hydronium and accelerates the interaction of free H atoms with each other increases the efficiency. One of the predicted results in this study is increased pressure. This effect is almost dissociative for hydronium. Another way to increase H production is to maintain regularity in the solution. To do this, the most optimal molecular groups must be used in appropriate amounts. Therefore, the amounts of NaOH, catalyst, and ammonia borane are tested in the study. Any molecular structure used in excess may lead to a decrease rather than an increase in H2. For example, using excess amounts of NaOH molecules creates Na saturation. This is a barrier that prevents interactions on the catalyst. Another estimated result is that using rhodium instead of ruthenium in the system contribute 30 % to the total H2 production. The agglomeration process of metal elements and the decrease in H2 production rate were seen with a detailed molecular dynamic. The possibility of increasing metal ions shifting from homogeneous distribution to heterogeneous distribution in catalysis was also shown in the molecular dynamics study. Unless the titriplex surface area increases, metal ions prefer to interact with each other. This suppresses existing H2 activation.
